data.title: Phillips 66 sending U.S. oil on foreign-flagged tanker after Jones Act waiver
data.description:
Phillips 66 (PSX) is shipping oil from Texas to a U.S. East Coast refinery, the first cargo of U.S. crude on that route under ‌a temporary Jones Act waiver issued by President Trump to hold down price increases and supply disruptions ​due to the Iran war, Bloomberg reported Thursday.

The Bakken crude was loaded earlier this month at a Phillips 66 (PSX) terminal in Beaumont, Texas, onto the Malta-flagged HTM Warrior, according to the report, which cited ship-tracking data from Kpler, and will be delivered to the Trainer refinery in Pennsylvania, owned by Delta Air Lines' (DAL) Monroe Energy unit.

The use of a foreign-flagged vessel to transport oil between U.S. ports was possible only because of a 60-day waiver Trump signed in March, effectively exempting commodities from the 1920 Jones Act that requires ships carrying goods between U.S. ports to be American-built, -flagged, and -operated.

Trump reportedly is considering another extension of his Jones Act waiver.

data.title: Comfort Systems tops estimates as data-center boom keeps backlog surging
data.description:
Comfort Systems USA (FIX), a contractor that installs and services heating, ventilation, air conditioning, plumbing and electrical systems for commercial and industrial buildings, reported results that easily beat Wall Street expectations as demand tied to data centers and large-scale construction remained strong.

Shares of Comfort Systems (FIX) rose 4.7% in extended trading Thursday. As of Wednesday’s close, the stock had gained 400% over the prior 12 months as investors bet on its role in the AI data center buildout.

Revenue in the first quarter rose to $2.87 billion from $1.83 billion a year earlier, topping analysts’ consensus estimate of $2.39 billion.

Net income rose to $370.4 million, or $10.51 a share, from $169.3 million, or $4.75 a share, a year earlier, while earnings topped analyst estimates of $6.81 a share.

Profit more than doubles

Operating income climbed to $485.7 million from $209.1 million, while margins expanded sharply as the company converted higher sales into outsized profit growth.

Comfort Systems (FIX) reported earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization of $524.4 million, up from $242.7 million a year earlier. Adjusted ebitda margin improved to 18.3% from 13.3%.

CEO highlights record momentum

“Our growing teams continue to achieve masterful performance across the United States,” Chief Executive Brian Lane said in the earnings release.

He added that the company generated organic revenue growth of 51% in the quarter and more than doubled per-share earnings from a year earlier.

Backlog keeps climbing

Comfort Systems (FIX) said backlog reached $12.45 billion at March 31, up from $11.94 billion at the end of December and $6.89 billion a year earlier. On a same-store basis, backlog increased to $12.21 billion.

Lane said recent bookings, persistent demand and a strong project pipeline support optimism for the next several quarters.

Known brands and footprint

Comfort Systems (FIX) operates through a network of local subsidiaries across 197 locations in 143 U.S. cities. Its regional operating brands include well-known specialty contractors such as TDIndustries, J&S Mechanical, Mesa Energy Systems, ShoffnerKalthoff MES and other local mechanical and electrical service businesses that handle mission-critical projects including hospitals, manufacturing plants and data centers.

data.title: MaxLinear Non-GAAP EPS of $0.22 beats by $0.04, revenue of $137.2M beats by $2.62M
data.description:
• MaxLinear press release (MXL): Q1 Non-GAAP EPS of $0.22 beats by $0.04.

  • Second Quarter 2026 Business Outlook

    The company expects net revenue in the second quarter of 2026 to be approximately $160 million to $170 million. The Company also estimates the following:

    • GAAP gross margin of approximately 56.0% to 59.0%;
    • Non-GAAP gross margin of approximately 58.0% to 61.0%;
    • GAAP operating expenses of approximately $91 million to $97 million;
    • Non-GAAP operating expenses of approximately $61 million to $66 million;
    • GAAP interest and other expense of approximately $1.8 million to $2.2 million;
    • Non-GAAP interest and other expense of approximately $1.8 million to $2.2 million;
    • GAAP income tax benefit of $2.0 million and non-GAAP income tax provision of $1.0 million, and
    • GAAP and non-GAAP diluted share count of approximately 95 million each.

data.title: Columbia Banking System Non-GAAP EPS of $0.72 beats by $0.03, revenue of $677M in-line
data.description:
• Columbia Banking System press release (COLB): Q1 Non-GAAP EPS of $0.72 beats by $0.03.
  • Revenue of $677M (+40.5% Y/Y) in-line.

  • Total consolidated assets were $66.0 billion as of March 31, 2026, compared to $66.8 billion as of December 31, 2025. The decrease reflects balance sheet optimization activity, which includes the reduction of excess cash. Cash and cash equivalents were $2.1 billion as of March 31, 2026, compared to $2.4 billion as of December 31, 2025. Including secured off-balance sheet lines of credit, total available liquidity was $27.1 billion as of March 31, 2026, representing 41% of total assets, 51% of total deposits, and 129% of uninsured deposits. Available-for-sale securities, which are held on balance sheet at fair value, were $10.9 billion as of March 31, 2026, compared to $11.1 billion as of December 31, 2025. The decrease is due to paydowns and a decrease in the fair value of the portfolio, partially offset by the purchase of $208 million of investment securities. Please refer to the Q1 2026 Earnings Presentation for additional details related to our investment securities portfolio and liquidity position.

data.title: Snap-on outlines $28M quarterly corporate costs and ~$100M 2026 capex amid tariff uncertainty
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Snap-on Incorporated (SNA) Q1 2026

Management view

  • Snap-on framed the quarter as a return to momentum despite macro turbulence, with Chairman, CEO & President Nicholas Pinchuk saying, "the resilience of our markets and the strength of our operations have restarted our momentum, registering strong sales" and adding that "through the blizzard, with uncertainty in tariffs, opposing currencies, rising material costs... our gross margins have resisted the impacts and overall profits have remained at a strong level."
  • Pinchuk highlighted stronger tool storage demand as a notable change in customer behavior, stating, "green shoots are popping up. Even with our tool storage units, they were up this quarter. I'll say that again. Tool storage was up," while also noting technician caution: "Tech confidence remains tepid, reticence toward long-term purchases. But they are bullish on shorter payback solutions."
  • Management emphasized continued technology investment in RS&I, with Pinchuk describing the push to apply "large language models and natural language translators" to proprietary databases and citing "our newly launched feature that streamlines the process for confronting job estimates" under the Mitchell 1 brand.
  • CFO Aldo Pagliari summarized profitability and cost drivers, saying, "Consolidated gross margin of 50.4% compared to 50.7% in the first quarter last year" and that benefits from volume and RCI were "largely offset by higher tariffs and other material costs" alongside "increased personnel costs and expanded technology investments."

Outlook

  • Pagliari provided the company’s explicit 2026 outlook items, stating, "With respect to corporate costs, we currently believe that expenses will approximate $28 million each quarter," "We expect that capital expenditures for the year will approximate $100 million," and "we currently anticipate that our full year 2026 effective income tax rate will be in a range of 22% to 23%."
  • Compared with the prior quarter’s call, the numerical outlook items for 2026 were reiterated at the same levels (corporate costs of about $28 million per quarter, capex about $100 million, tax rate 22% to 23%), while this quarter added a reminder that "in the third quarter of 2025, earnings per share included a $0.31 nonrecurring onetime benefit from the RS&I Group legal settlement."

Financial results

  • Snap-on reported first-quarter net sales of $1.2072 billion and EPS of $4.69, with Pagliari stating, "Net sales of $1.207.2 billion in the quarter represented an increase of 5.8% from 2025 levels," and "Net earnings of $247 million or $4.69 per diluted share compared to $240.5 million or $4.51 per diluted share in 2025."
  • Segment mix shifted toward stronger Tools profitability and softer RS&I earnings, as Pagliari said Snap-on Tools gross margin "improved 140 basis points to 47.7%" and operating margin was "21.6%," while RS&I operating earnings were "$119.5 million compared to $122.1 million last year" with operating margin "24.6%."
  • Financial Services showed lower revenue and earnings year over year, with Pagliari stating, "Financial Services revenue of $101.1 million... compared to $102.1 million last year, while operating earnings of $68 million compared to $70.3 million in 2025," and attributing lower revenue to "lower interest income resulting from a year-over-year decrease in the size of the average portfolio."
  • Cash flow and capital deployment were highlighted, with Pagliari reporting, "Cash provided by operating activities of $368.7 million in the quarter represented 145% of net earnings," dividends of $126.8 million, and "the repurchase of 267,000 shares... for $99.9 million," with $234.1 million remaining authorization.

Q&A

  • Bret Jordan, Jefferies: asked whether heavy-duty strength was cyclical vs. product-driven; Chairman & CEO Pinchuk said, "we are understanding the work around heavy duty more every day... and... we're also capturing some share in our business."
  • Jordan, Jefferies: asked about “green shoots” and quarter cadence; Pinchuk said the signal was "principally" tool storage and recent franchisee conversations, adding, "franchisees do not hold back when they talk to me... And these conversations were pretty positive."
  • Scott Stember, ROTH: asked for category performance; Pinchuk said, "hand tools was up. Power tools was up. Diagnostics was tepid." Stember also pressed on tariffs and refunds; Pinchuk said, "We don't think tariffs are going to change very much actually going forward," and Pagliari added, "They opened the portal for people to apply for refunds... our strategy is to protect... we don't want anything to expire."
  • Luke Junk, Baird: asked about tax rebates affecting demand/finance; Pinchuk said, "I don't think we're seeing it as a big factor," while noting credit metrics and that "originations were kind of flattish." Junk also asked about military exposure and lag; Pinchuk said, "We expect the military to improve going forward. We truly do."
  • Christopher Glynn, Oppenheimer: asked whether C&I improvements reflected structural capability; Pinchuk emphasized share gains and execution, saying, "I really do believe we're gaining share because our products are getting better" and attributed improvement to "capacity" and faster delivery. Glynn also asked if originations could grow; Pinchuk replied, "I chose [green shoots] particularly because I'm not sure what the increase in tool storage means... we'll see how that plays out."
  • Gary Prestopino, Barrington: asked how much tool storage was up; Pinchuk declined to quantify, saying, "tool storage was up more than the average." Prestopino also asked about data center demand; Pinchuk answered, "Yes, we're seeing increased demand" and "being asked to quote." On FX impact to EPS, Pagliari said, "$0.02 of good news."

Sentiment analysis

  • Analyst tone was neutral to slightly skeptical, with repeated probing on whether C&I momentum is cyclical, whether tool storage/originations are truly turning, and how tariffs/refunds could affect results.
  • Management tone was slightly positive but cautious, pairing confidence statements with uncertainty qualifiers; Pinchuk said, "we proceed with confidence" yet also, "it's 1 quarter" and "it's unsure what will happen with tariff refunds and when they will be paid."
  • Versus the prior quarter, management language kept the same “turbulence” framing ("The hits just keep on coming" appeared in both calls), but Q1 introduced more “green shoots” emphasis tied to tool storage and franchisee conversations, while maintaining a no-guidance posture ("We don't give guidance").

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Sales growth accelerated in Q1 versus Q4 on an organic basis (Q1: 3.4% organic; Q4: 1.4% organic), while consolidated margins were described as pressured by FX and investment in both periods (Q1: 40 bps FX headwind cited; Q4: higher material costs/mix and investment).
  • Tools Group shifted from slight organic decline in Q4 to organic growth in Q1, and management’s narrative moved from "tool storage remains the most impacted category" (Q4) to "tool storage was up" (Q1), without providing a quantified rate.
  • RS&I commentary shifted from Q4’s growth with OEMs/dealerships to Q1’s mix pressure, with Pinchuk stating Q1 RS&I was offset by "lower sales to OEM dealerships" and describing EQS as having "hit a flat spot."
  • Analysts’ focus remained consistent on Tools mix/diagnostics, C&I durability, and technician demand signals, while Q1 added pointed questions on tariff refund mechanics and U.S. tax rebate effects.

Risks and concerns

  • Management identified ongoing macro headwinds, with Pinchuk citing "uncertainty in tariffs," "opposing currencies," and "rising material costs," and later adding "unfavorable currency diluting our margins" and "the fluctuation in government policies."
  • Tariff outcomes and refunds were framed as uncertain, with Pinchuk saying, "we're not depending on anything out of this" and emphasizing execution and rights-protection via filings.
  • RS&I profitability risk was linked to investment and FX, with Pinchuk attributing lower margin to "unfavorable currency and our investments fortifying our proprietary database."

Final takeaway

Snap-on management described Q1 as a return to stronger momentum, citing record first-quarter sales of $1.2072 billion and EPS of $4.69 while emphasizing resilience against tariffs, FX, and material costs. The most notable demand signal discussed was a year-over-year increase in tool storage, which leadership repeatedly framed as “green shoots” rather than a confirmed trend, alongside continued technician preference for short-payback purchases. For 2026, the company reiterated expected corporate costs of about $28 million per quarter, about $100 million of capex, and a 22% to 23% effective tax rate, while continuing investment in proprietary data and large language model initiatives in RS&I.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Eagle Bancorp forecasts 2026 NIM of 2.6%-2.8% while maintaining balance sheet repositioning
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Eagle Bancorp (EGBN) Q1 2026

Management View

  • CEO Susan Riel said the company started 2026 “on track with our near-term strategic priorities: generating capital through earnings, diversifying the balance sheet across both assets and funding and executing on the repositioning work,” adding, “We returned to profitability, expanded net interest margin and delivered strong C&I growth.”

  • Riel emphasized that near-term earnings headwinds are tied to runoff and resolutions, saying, “The pace at which legacy exposures resolve and scheduled payoffs occur is faster than the pace at which we can prudently generate new earning assets,” while also stressing, “We are not shrinking the balance sheet because deposits are leaving us.”

  • CFO Eric Newell highlighted a return to profitability and framed it as tied to credit actions: “This quarter, we reported net income of $14.7 million or $0.48 per diluted share, a meaningful swing from the $2.4 million loss we reported last quarter.”

  • Newell detailed credit and concentration progress, including: “Our CRE concentration ratio… declined to 295% at March 31, moving below the 300% threshold,” and “Our ADC concentration ratio came in at 76%.”

  • On criticized/classified trends and remaining pressure points, Newell said, “Balances declined by $79.9 million in the quarter to $794.1 million,” but also noted, “$159.9 million of downgrades occurred in the first quarter.”

Outlook

  • CFO Eric Newell said, “Our 2026 forecast is substantially unchanged from what we shared last quarter,” and reiterated key targets: “We continue to expect full year NIM in the 2.6% to 2.8% range; noninterest income growth of 15% to 25%; and noninterest expense flat to down 4% when adjusting for the notable items I mentioned.”

  • Newell maintained the repositioning message on volumes: “Average deposits, loans and earning assets are still expected to decline year-over-year, reflecting intentional balance sheet repositioning rather than operating pressure.”

  • On Q2-to-back-half balance sheet dynamics discussed in Q&A, Newell said, “Our expectation is that CRE will continue to see some decline in the second quarter,” while also projecting, “When you compare year-end '25 to year-end '26 for the CRE portfolio, it will be flat.”

Financial Results

  • CFO Eric Newell summarized key earnings drivers, stating, “Net interest income declined $4.6 million to $63.7 million,” and “NIM expanded 9 basis points to 2.47%, driven by an improved funding mix as wholesale funding usage declined.”

  • Newell flagged the expense normalization versus the prior quarter, saying, “Noninterest expense… declined $21.1 million to $48.7 million,” reflecting “the absence of 2 notable items from the fourth quarter, $14.7 million of expenses related to loan dispositions and a $10 million legal provision tied to the probable and estimable resolution of our previously disclosed government investigation.”

  • On credit costs and reserves, Newell reported, “Provision for credit losses totaled $13.4 million,” “Net charge-offs totaled $26 million,” and “Our allowance for credit losses ended the quarter at $147.2 million or 2.12% of total loans,” adding, “Within that total, we carry $60 million of reserves, specifically against our income-producing office portfolio.”

  • On funding and liquidity actions, Newell said, “Period-end deposits declined $542 million… of which, $413 million reflected the intentional reduction of brokered deposits,” while also noting, “Available liquidity stands at $4.3 billion, and we maintain close to 2x coverage of uninsured deposits.”

Q&A

  • Justin Crowley, Piper Sandler: Asked whether criticized/classified inflows should slow and how to think about the trajectory; Senior Executive Vice President Ryan Riel answered, “Forecasting what that is, is tough to do,” and CFO Eric Newell added, “Our goal and commitment… is that criticized and classified will continue to come down on an absolute level.”

  • Crowley, Piper Sandler: Asked about future loan sales beyond held-for-sale and whether disposals will move outside office; Ryan Riel responded, “We’re looking at each and every case on a one-off basis,” and said, “The anticipation is that tactic will continue to be used.”

  • Crowley, Piper Sandler: Asked about the office reserve reduction; CFO Eric Newell said, “The biggest driver… comes due to the $37-ish million reduction of substandard loans,” and added, “We believe that the $60 million… is appropriate at March 31.”

  • David Chiaverini, Jefferies: Asked if commentary about improvement also applies to nonaccruals; Newell replied, “Yes, generally,” and said, “As that portfolio continues to decline… the incidence… of some of those loans flowing into nonaccrual or charge-off will also fall.”

  • Chiaverini, Jefferies: Asked about held-for-sale pricing and percent of par; Ryan Riel said, “It’s been significant, frankly, drop from par on the office side,” and Newell added, “For the office portfolio, we’ve been probably between 45% and 50%.”

  • Catherine Mealor, KBW: Pressed on balance sheet size versus unchanged guidance; Newell said, “We maintained the forecast,” citing that “the forward curve at March 31 has largely priced out the 2… reductions… expected at year-end,” and also noting seasonal average-balance impacts from “a third-party payment processor.”

  • Stephen Moss, Raymond James: Asked about near-term maturities for the downgraded hotel and multifamily credits; Ryan Riel said, “We did put short-term extensions in place in both of those situations,” and added, “We continue to work with each of those clients for a longer-term solution.”

  • Christopher Marinac, Brean: Asked whether smaller credits could reduce inflow risk and how reserves may trend; Ryan Riel answered, “Short answer is yes,” while Newell said, “The first quarter provision expense as well as charge-offs, that’s a decent run rate for our expectation for the remainder of the year.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts: Slightly negative to neutral, with recurring pressure on credit visibility and balance sheet shrinkage (e.g., Mealor’s “do you feel like… the range… will fall” and Chiaverini’s “at what percent of par… are you selling loans”).

  • Management: Slightly positive in prepared remarks and more measured in Q&A, with confidence paired with caution (e.g., CEO Susan Riel: “The first quarter demonstrated that our strategy is working,” while CFO Eric Newell: “We’re encouraged by the trajectory,” but also, “this work is not finished”).

  • Versus last quarter: Management language stayed consistent on repositioning, but sounded more upbeat on profitability and NIM; analysts remained focused on credit migration and resolution execution, similar to Q4.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Profitability swung quarter-to-quarter, with CFO Eric Newell reporting Q1 net income of $14.7 million vs. Q4 net income of $7.6 million, while emphasizing in Q1 that the uplift “reflects the hard work underway across the portfolio.”

  • Credit mix shifted: Q1 nonperforming loans rose to $128.8 million (1.86% of total loans) vs. Q4 nonperforming loans of $106.8 million (1.47%), while Q1 criticized/classified (including held-for-sale) declined to $794.1 million vs. Q4 special mention and substandard loans of $783.4 million.

  • Guidance framing remained aligned: in Q1 Newell said the 2026 forecast is “substantially unchanged,” consistent with Q4’s positioning that “we’re forecasting a meaningful expansion in net interest margin with NIM expected to range between 2.6% and 2.8% for the year.”

  • Analyst focus was similar in both quarters—held-for-sale execution, office valuations/reserves, and the pace of criticized/classified reduction—while Q1 added more probing on near-term balance sheet levels against guidance.

Risks and Concerns

  • CEO Susan Riel highlighted an earnings risk from asset runoff, saying, “That asymmetry creates near-term pressure on net interest income and a smaller earning asset base.”

  • CFO Eric Newell pointed to ongoing credit work and elevated resolution impacts, saying, “Nonperforming loans increased,” and “active resolution of problem credits is producing elevated charge-offs.”

  • Management emphasized portfolio-specific risk items, including Newell’s disclosure that “three relationships accounted for the majority of the downgrade activity,” while stating, “Taken together, these are discrete situations.”

Final Takeaway

Management described Q1 as an inflection toward execution: returning to profitability, widening NIM, and advancing balance sheet repositioning through brokered-deposit reduction and accelerated credit resolutions. The company kept its full-year 2026 targets unchanged (including a 2.6%-2.8% NIM range), while repeatedly acknowledging that faster legacy runoff than new-asset generation and ongoing nonaccrual/charge-off activity can pressure near-term net interest income as Eagle works toward “a cleaner balance sheet” and higher pre-provision net revenue.
